categories.name: JavaScript Programming
categories.description: Language specifics, object management, and syntax rules.

topics.name: Object Property Access
topics.description: Methods for retrieving data from JavaScript objects, including dot, bracket, and destructuring patterns.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a restriction for a property name to be used as a valid identifier with the dot property accessor?
questions.explanation: An identifier in JavaScript can contain Unicode letters, $, _, and digits 0..9, but it cannot start with a digit.
questions.hint: Think about what character cannot be at the very beginning.

source.title: 3 Ways To Access Object Properties in JavaScript

choices.text: It cannot contain the underscore (_) character.
It cannot start with a digit (0..9).
It must be at least 3 characters long.

questions.question_text: Why does the expression 'weirdObject.prop-3' evaluate to NaN instead of retrieving the property value 'three'?
questions.explanation: Because 'prop-3' is an invalid identifier for dot access, JavaScript interprets the dot as property access to 'prop' (which is undefined) and then attempts to subtract 3 from it, resulting in Not-a-Number (NaN).
questions.hint: Consider how JavaScript interprets the hyphen (-) character in this context.

source.title: 3 Ways To Access Object Properties in JavaScript

questions.answer_text: JavaScript interprets the expression as a subtraction operation (object.prop minus 3) because the hyphen makes it an invalid identifier.

questions.question_text: In the square brackets property accessor syntax 'expression[expression]', what must the second expression evaluate to?
questions.explanation: The first expression evaluates to the object, and the second expression must evaluate to a string that denotes the property name.
questions.hint: Property names are ultimately treated as what data type?

source.title: 3 Ways To Access Object Properties in JavaScript

choices.text: A string denoting the property name.
A reference to the window object.

questions.question_text: What is the correct syntax to extract a property into a variable with a different name (aliasing) using destructuring?
questions.explanation: The syntax 'const { identifier: aliasIdentifier } = expression' allows you to map the property name to a new variable name.
questions.hint: The colon separates the property name from the new variable name.

source.title: 3 Ways To Access Object Properties in JavaScript

questions.question_text: Which syntax is used to perform object destructuring when the property name is dynamic and determined at runtime?
questions.explanation: To destructure a dynamic property, you must wrap the expression in square brackets inside the destructuring pattern: { [expression]: identifier }.
questions.hint: It combines square bracket logic inside the destructuring braces.

source.title: 3 Ways To Access Object Properties in JavaScript

questions.question_text: True or False: In JavaScript, if you attempt to access a property that does not exist using any of the three accessor syntaxes, the code will throw a ReferenceError.
questions.explanation: If the accessed property doesn't exist, all 3 accessor syntaxes (dot, brackets, and destructuring) simply evaluate to 'undefined', they do not throw an error.
questions.hint: Think about the standard value for 'nothing found' in an object.

source.title: 3 Ways To Access Object Properties in JavaScript

learning_cards.title: Dot vs Bracket Access
learning_cards.front: When should you choose square brackets over the dot property accessor?
learning_cards.back: When the property name is dynamic (determined at runtime) or when the property name is not a valid identifier (e.g., starts with a digit or contains a hyphen).

source.title: 3 Ways To Access Object Properties in JavaScript

learning_cards.title: Purpose of Destructuring
learning_cards.front: When is it most appropriate to use object destructuring for property access?
learning_cards.back: When you want to extract a property value directly into a standalone variable.

source.title: 3 Ways To Access Object Properties in JavaScript
